original_abstract:
Plant-derived exosome-like nanovesicles (PDENs) offer significant potential for disease treatment and targeted drug delivery. However, their clinical standardization is currently hindered by limited knowledge regarding their biogenesis, molecular markers, and the lack of efficient, large-scale production methods. 

In this study, we isolated novel PDEN-based immunomodulators, termed CLDENs, from the apoplastic fluid of *Catharanthus roseus* (L.) Don leaves. These membrane-bound vesicles averaged 75.51 nm in size with a stable surface charge of −21.8 mV. CLDENs demonstrated remarkable robustness, maintaining structural integrity across extreme pH ranges, enzymatic challenges, and simulated gastrointestinal conditions. Biodistribution assays confirmed that CLDENs are internalized by immune cells and successfully target immune organs following intraperitoneal injection. Lipidomic profiling underscored their unique chemistry—notably a 36.5% ether-phospholipid content—while proteomics identified six specific marker proteins and suggested a biogenetic origin in multivesicular bodies.

Functionally, CLDENs promoted macrophage polarization, enhanced phagocytosis, and stimulated lymphocyte proliferation in vitro. In vivo, CLDENs (20–60 mg/kg) effectively reversed cyclophosphamide-induced immunosuppression by alleviating leucopenia and bone marrow cell cycle arrest. Mechanistically, CLDENs triggered a potent immune response via the TNF-α/NF-κB signaling axis and the hematopoietic transcription factor PU.1. To address the challenge of scalability, we established a *C. roseus* cell culture system. This platform yielded gram-level quantities of nanovesicles—tripling the efficiency of traditional leaf extraction—while maintaining identical physical and biological properties. Our findings establish CLDENs as a biocompatible nano-biomaterial well-suited for adjuvant therapy in post-chemotherapy recovery.
humanized_abstract:
Plant-derived exosome-like nanovesicles (PDENs) hold promise for disease therapy and targeted drug delivery. While PDENs have remarkable biomedical applications, their translation to clinical standardization is obstructed due to the paucity of information regarding their biogenesis, their specific molecular markers and viable, large-scale production methods.

In this work, a series of new PDEN derived immunomodulatory molecules, CLDENs, were purified from the apoplastic fluid of the leaves of *Catharanthus roseus* (L.) Don. Size analysis revealed that these membrane-bound vesicles are approximately 75.51 nm in diameter with a surface charge of −21.8 mV and were highly stable in a wide pH range, under enzymatic digestion and under simulated gastrointestinal digestion. Ex vivo biodistribution analysis showed that these CLDENs are uptaken by immune cells and targeted the immune organs when injected intraperitoneally. Lipidomic analysis revealed a high content of ether-phospholipids, corresponding to 36.5% of the total lipidome. Proteomics identified six specific marker proteins, indicating their possible biogenetic origin from multivesicular bodies.

CLDENs promoted macrophage polarization and phagocytosis as well as stimulated lymphocyte proliferation in vitro. In vivo, CLDENs injection (20–60 mg/kg) restored immunogenicity following cyclophosphamide-mediated immunosuppression through partial recovery of leucopenia and re-entry of bone marrow cells into cycle. This immunostimulatory effect of CLDENs was associated with the activation of the TNF-α/NF-κB pathway and up-regulation of the haematopoietic transcription factor PU.1. To address the issue of large-scale production, we engineered a cell culture system derived from *Catharanthus roseus* leaves. Cell culture-derived nanovesicles (CLDENs) afforded significantly higher yields (>gram-scale) than their wild-type counterparts and retained indistinguishable morphological and functional features. Thus, CLDENs appear to be a biocompatible nano-biomaterial adapted for use as immunoadjuvants to enhance post-chemotherapeutic recovery.
